category_summary:
A high-stakes policy debate, multiple major product launches, and an open-source crisis dominated AI social media today.

- **Sam Altman** [disclosed amendments](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-b75b6f62b127) to **OpenAI's** Department of War contract, adding explicit Fourth Amendment protections against domestic surveillance and excluding intelligence agencies — sparking intense legal and ethical scrutiny. **Jeremy Howard** provided detailed legal analysis warning the language still has loopholes.
- **Anthropic** [rolled out voice mode](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-3274d91897f8) in **Claude Code** (push-to-talk, no extra cost), while reporting [unprecedented traffic growth](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-f36cca22ef34) that strained infrastructure. **Boris Cherny** [confirmed](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-cc97a5e8cfb2) the team is working around the clock to stabilize.
- **Google DeepMind** launched **Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite** with aggressive pricing ($0.25/1M input tokens) and 2.5X speed improvements, announced by **Jeff Dean**, **Logan Kilpatrick**, and others. **OpenAI** separately [teased **GPT-5.4**](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-f4ecc1eefc2d) coming soon and [rolled out **GPT-5.3 Instant**](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-d966512a53f7) to all ChatGPT users.
- A major [staff exodus](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-e8633a60877f) from **Alibaba's Qwen** team alarmed the open-source community. **Nato Lambert** [warned the collapse](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-f20c2053b909) would leave a gaping hole in the research ecosystem, especially for small models. **Ethan Mollick** offered [an influential framework](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-4742b9acbd2b) identifying four major AI capability leaps, while **Swyx** argued eliminating human code review is the "final boss" of agentic engineering.
category_summary_html:
A high-stakes policy debate, multiple major product launches, and an open-source crisis dominated AI social media today.

  • Sam Altman disclosed amendments to OpenAI's Department of War contract, adding explicit Fourth Amendment protections against domestic surveillance and excluding intelligence agencies — sparking intense legal and ethical scrutiny. Jeremy Howard provided detailed legal analysis warning the language still has loopholes.
  • Anthropic rolled out voice mode in Claude Code (push-to-talk, no extra cost), while reporting unprecedented traffic growth that strained infrastructure. Boris Cherny confirmed the team is working around the clock to stabilize.
  • Google DeepMind launched Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite with aggressive pricing ($0.25/1M input tokens) and 2.5X speed improvements, announced by Jeff Dean, Logan Kilpatrick, and others. OpenAI separately teased GPT-5.4 coming soon and rolled out GPT-5.3 Instant to all ChatGPT users.
  • A major staff exodus from Alibaba's Qwen team alarmed the open-source community. Nato Lambert warned the collapse would leave a gaping hole in the research ecosystem, especially for small models. Ethan Mollick offered an influential framework identifying four major AI capability leaps, while Swyx argued eliminating human code review is the "final boss" of agentic engineering.

themes.name: OpenAI-Department of War Contract Controversy
themes.description: Sam Altman shared internal details about amendments to OpenAI's DoW agreement, adding explicit Fourth Amendment protections against domestic surveillance, excluding intelligence agencies, and admitting the Friday announcement was poorly handled. Massive public discourse.

themes.name: Claude Code Voice Mode Launch
themes.description: Anthropic rolling out push-to-talk voice mode in Claude Code, allowing users to mix typed and spoken input. No extra cost, tokens don't count against rate limits. Available on paid plans.

themes.name: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Launch
themes.description: Google/DeepMind officially launched Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite, their most cost-efficient Gemini 3 model. Key specs: $0.25/1M input tokens, 2.5X faster TTFT than 2.5 Flash, 1432 Elo on LMArena, adjustable thinking levels. Multiple Google leaders (Jeff Dean, Noam Shazeer, Oriol Vinyals) co-announced.

themes.name: Qwen Team Exodus
themes.description: Major staff departures from Alibaba's Qwen team following restructuring, with 'Qwen is nothing without its people' messaging echoing OpenAI's 2023 drama. Community deeply concerned about impact on open-source AI ecosystem, especially small models.

themes.name: OpenAI GPT-5.3 Instant Rollout & 5.4 Teaser
themes.description: OpenAI launched GPT-5.3 Instant to all ChatGPT users ('more accurate, less cringe') and teased GPT-5.4 coming soon. The 5.3 Instant addresses user complaints about unnecessary refusals and preachy tone.

themes.name: Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite Release
themes.description: Google DeepMind launches Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite, reportedly beating Gemini 2.5 Flash on many tasks. Emphasis on speed and efficiency at the intelligence frontier.

themes.name: AI Progress Framework & Competitive Landscape
themes.description: Ethan Mollick proposed an influential four-leap framework for AI progress (ChatGPT → GPT-4 → Reasoners/o3 → Agentic systems) and mapped competitive differentiators across labs: Claude Cowork, NotebookLM, GPT-5.2 Pro as unique offerings. Noted harness/UX as key differentiator.

themes.name: Agentic Engineering & Code Review Automation
themes.description: Growing discussion about removing human code review bottleneck from AI agent workflows, with swyx's viral post on 'killing the code review' as the final boss of agentic engineering. LangChain's agent debugging example provides practical context.

themes.name: Claude Code Rapid Growth & Voice Mode
themes.description: Boris Cherny (Claude Code lead) reveals voice-mode coding for CLI, reports insane demand straining services, and ships rapid performance fixes. The product is clearly experiencing explosive adoption.

themes.name: Claude Unprecedented Growth & Scaling Challenges
themes.description: Anthropic reporting genuinely hard-to-forecast growth in Claude and Claude Code traffic, leading to instability and bugs. Engineers publicly acknowledging issues and working on fixes.

items.title:
Here is re-post of an internal post:

We have been working with the DoW to make some additions in ou...
items.content:
Here is re-post of an internal post:

We have been working with the DoW to make some additions in our agreement to make our principles very clear.

1.   We are going to amend our deal to add this language, in addition to everything else:

"• Consistent with applicable laws, including the Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution, National Security Act of 1947, FISA Act of 1978, the AI system shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and nationals.

• For the avoidance of doubt, the Department understands this limitation to prohibit deliberate tracking, surveillance, or monitoring of U.S. persons or nationals, including through the procurement or use of commercially acquired personal or identifiable information."

It’s critical to protect the civil liberties of Americans, and there was so much focus on this, that we wanted to make this point especially clear, including around commercially acquired information. Just like everything we do with iterative deployment, we will continue to learn and refine as we go.

I think this is an important change; our team and the DoW team did a great job working on it.

2. The Department also affirmed that our services will not be used by Department of War intelligence agencies (for example, the NSA). Any services to those agencies would require a follow-on modification to our contract.

3. For extreme clarity: we want to work through democratic processes. It should be the government making the key decisions about society. We want to have a voice, and a seat at the table where we can share our expertise, and to fight for principles of liberty. But we are clear on how the system works (because a lot of people have asked, if I received what I believed was an unconstitutional order, of course I would rather go to jail than follow it). But

4. There are many things the technology just isn’t ready for, and many areas we don’t yet understand the tradeoffs required for safety. We will work through these, slowly, with the DoW, with technical safeguards and other methods.

5. One thing I think I did wrong: we shouldn't have rushed to get this out on Friday. The issues are super complex, and demand clear communication. We were genuinely trying to de-escalate things and avoid a much worse outcome, but I think it just looked opportunistic and sloppy. Good learning experience for me as we face higher-stakes decisions in the future.

In my conversations over the weekend, I reiterated that Anthropic should not be designated as a SCR, and that we hope the DoW offers them the same terms we’ve agreed to.

We will host an All Hands tomorrow morning to answer more questions.

items.content_html:
Here is re-post of an internal post:

We have been working with the DoW to make some additions in our agreement to make our principles very clear.

1. We are going to amend our deal to add this language, in addition to everything else:

"• Consistent with applicable laws, including the Fourth Amendment to the United States Constitution, National Security Act of 1947, FISA Act of 1978, the AI system shall not be intentionally used for domestic surveillance of U.S. persons and nationals.

• For the avoidance of doubt, the Department understands this limitation to prohibit deliberate tracking, surveillance, or monitoring of U.S. persons or nationals, including through the procurement or use of commercially acquired personal or identifiable information."

It’s critical to protect the civil liberties of Americans, and there was so much focus on this, that we wanted to make this point especially clear, including around commercially acquired information. Just like everything we do with iterative deployment, we will continue to learn and refine as we go.

I think this is an important change; our team and the DoW team did a great job working on it.

2. The Department also affirmed that our services will not be used by Department of War intelligence agencies (for example, the NSA). Any services to those agencies would require a follow-on modification to our contract.

3. For extreme clarity: we want to work through democratic processes. It should be the government making the key decisions about society. We want to have a voice, and a seat at the table where we can share our expertise, and to fight for principles of liberty. But we are clear on how the system works (because a lot of people have asked, if I received what I believed was an unconstitutional order, of course I would rather go to jail than follow it). But

4. There are many things the technology just isn’t ready for, and many areas we don’t yet understand the tradeoffs required for safety. We will work through these, slowly, with the DoW, with technical safeguards and other methods.

5. One thing I think I did wrong: we shouldn't have rushed to get this out on Friday. The issues are super complex, and demand clear communication. We were genuinely trying to de-escalate things and avoid a much worse outcome, but I think it just looked opportunistic and sloppy. Good learning experience for me as we face higher-stakes decisions in the future.

In my conversations over the weekend, I reiterated that Anthropic should not be designated as a SCR, and that we hope the DoW offers them the same terms we’ve agreed to.

We will host an All Hands tomorrow morning to answer more questions.

items.title: ⚡ Excited to announce Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite! We’ve set a new standard for efficiency and capability ...
items.content:
⚡ Excited to announce Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite! We’ve set a new standard for efficiency and capability to give developers our fastest, most cost-effective Gemini 3 model yet.

We engineered this model with thinking levels, allowing it to handle high-volume queries instantly, while scaling up its reasoning for complex edge cases.

By the numbers:
⏱️ 2.5X faster time-to-first-token than 2.5 Flash while being significantly higher quality
📉 $0.25 per 1M input tokens
📊 1432 Elo on LMArena & 86.9% on GPQA Diamond

Thrilled to see what developers build with this kind of speed and quality at scale. Available now in Google AI Studio and Vertex AI.

https://t.co/Weal73Juh8

items.title:
(I also would like to share this, which I wrote after thinking a little more.)

There is a lot we wi...
items.content:
(I also would like to share this, which I wrote after thinking a little more.)

There is a lot we will talk about in the coming days, but since this is one of the first "real deal" decisions we have faced, I wanted to share a few things that have been heavily on my mind the past few days.

These are the principles I care most about for this decision: alignment, democratization, empowerment, and individual agency.

The democratic process must stay in control, and we must democratize AI. OpenAI should not decide the fate of the world; no private company should. We need to work with governments, but also we need to make sure individuals get increasing power.

Things are moving so fast that we need to urgently educate the world so that the democratic process has time to catch up. I think one of our most important strategic decisions ever was the principle of iterative deployment.

In particular, the key element required for democracy, such as protection of privacy, must be defended by all of society.

I believe that, as some of the creators of this new technology, we deserve to and are obligated to have a loud voice about the risks, pitfalls, and benefits we see. 

I think we are heading towards a world where the relationship between governments and AI efforts is critical. This will be difficult but it has to happen; I do not see any good future where we don't get there. There should not be games and fights in the press like this; drastic government action should be avoided.

I think there are real dangers coming to the world, and maybe pretty soon; I tried to put myself in the mindset of how I'd feel the day after an attack on the US or a new bioweapon we could have helped prevent.

items.title:
From an AI user perspective, the four big leaps so far in ability: 
1. GPT-3.5 (ChatGPT, November 20...
items.content:
From an AI user perspective, the four big leaps so far in ability: 
1. GPT-3.5 (ChatGPT, November 2022)
2. GPT-4 (Spring 2023)
3. Reasoners (starts with o1-preview, but the real deal was o3, Spring 2025)
4. Workable agentic systems (Harness + good reasoner models, December 2025)
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Mollick's influential framework: Four big AI capability leaps — (1) ChatGPT/GPT-3.5 (Nov 2022), (2) GPT-4 (Spring 2023), (3) Reasoners/o3 (Spring 2025), (4) Workable agentic systems (Dec 2025).
items.reasoning: Extremely high engagement (2344 likes, 209K views). Influential framing of AI progress from one of the most-followed AI commentators. Establishes a useful taxonomy of AI capability leaps that will likely shape discourse.

From an AI user perspective, the four big leaps so far in ability:

1. GPT-3.5 (ChatGPT, November 2022)

2. GPT-4 (Spring 2023)

3. Reasoners (starts with o1-preview, but the real deal was o3, Spring 2025)

4. Workable agentic systems (Harness + good reasoner models, December 2025)

killing the Code Review

items.content:
this is the Final Boss of Agentic Engineering: 

killing the Code Review

at this point multiple people are already weighing how to remove the human code review bottleneck from agents becoming fully productive. @ankitxg was brave enough to map out how he sees SDLC being turned on its head.

i'm not personally there yet, but I tend to be 3-6 months behind these people and yeah its definitely coming.

items.title: @j_asminewang OK here's the informal/unofficial/etc answer from our law firm CEO -- tldr, this langu...
items.author: @jeremyphoward
items.summary: Jeremy Howard shares legal analysis of OpenAI's government contract: the word 'intentionally' sets a high bar for proving misuse, 'applicable laws' is a moving target, and the contract doesn't extend to negligence or recklessness standards.
items.reasoning: Substantive legal analysis from well-known AI researcher (253 likes, 31K views) with input from a law firm CEO. Important for AI governance/policy discourse.
items.summary_html: Jeremy Howard shares legal analysis of OpenAI's government contract: the word 'intentionally' sets a high bar for proving misuse, 'applicable laws' is a moving target, and the contract doesn't extend to negligence or recklessness standards.
items.content_html:
@j_asminewang OK here's the informal/unofficial/etc answer from our law firm CEO -- tldr, this language doesn't seem to add much to the previously shared contract details:

"shall not be used" is constrained to be where that's "Consistent with applicable laws". And "the Department understands this limitation to prohibit" doesn’t do much lifting. It may help with a courts interpretation because a contract requires a “meeting of the minds,” but it still doesn’t negate the fact that “applicable laws” is a moving target.

I’m concerned/surprised that the bar doesn’t extend to negligence or at minimum recklessness. The hierarchy of mens rea is purposely > knowingly > recklessly > negligently and courts often read "intentionally" to be somewhere in between "purposely" and "knowingly." "intentionally" is a higher bar and more difficult to prove than recklessness or negligence.

items.title:
Smarter. Faster. Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is here⚡

The model offers uncompromising speed & intelligenc...
items.content:
Smarter. Faster. Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is here⚡

The model offers uncompromising speed & intelligence  at scale by focusing on:

— Cost-efficiency: Priced at just $0.25/1M input and $1.50/1M output tokens, it gets work done faster at a fraction of the cost of larger models, including a 45% increase in output speed compared to Gemini 2.5 Flash

— Compute control: 3.1 Flash-Lite comes with flexible thinking levels so you can actively select how much the model “thinks” for a given task, based on the complexity of your project

— Scalable intelligence for building: The model can handle high volume  tasks at scale while keeping costs down *and* can tackle complex workloads where in-depth reasoning is needed, like generating user interfaces or detailed instruction following

3.1 Flash-Lite is rolling out in preview today via the Gemini API in @GoogleAIStudio and Vertex AI.

Smarter. Faster. Gemini 3.1 Flash-Lite is here⚡

The model offers uncompromising speed & intelligence at scale by focusing on:

— Cost-efficiency: Priced at just $0.25/1M input and $1.50/1M output tokens, it gets work done faster at a fraction of the cost of larger models, including a 45% increase in output speed compared to Gemini 2.5 Flash

— Compute control: 3.1 Flash-Lite comes with flexible thinking levels so you can actively select how much the model “thinks” for a given task, based on the complexity of your project

— Scalable intelligence for building: The model can handle high volume tasks at scale while keeping costs down *and* can tackle complex workloads where in-depth reasoning is needed, like generating user interfaces or detailed instruction following

3.1 Flash-Lite is rolling out in preview today via the Gemini API in @GoogleAIStudio and Vertex AI.

The transcript streams in at your cursor position. You can type half a prompt, voice the messy middle part, keep typing. It doesn't replace what's already there.

1. GPT-3.5 (ChatGPT, November 20...
items.content:
From an AI user perspective, the four big leaps so far in ability: 
1. GPT-3.5 (ChatGPT, November 2022)
2. GPT-4 (Spring 2023)
3. Reasoners (starts with o1-preview, but the real deal was o3, Spring 2025)
4. Workable agentic systems (Harness + good reasoner models, December 2025)
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social

items.reasoning: Excellent historical taxonomy from a top AI analyst. The identification of 'agentic systems + harness' as the fourth major leap is significant and forward-looking. High engagement (85 likes on Bluesky is strong). This framework is useful for understanding where we are in AI development.

items.content_html:
From an AI user perspective, the four big leaps so far in ability:

1. GPT-3.5 (ChatGPT, November 2022)

2. GPT-4 (Spring 2023)

3. Reasoners (starts with o1-preview, but the real deal was o3, Spring 2025)

4. Workable agentic systems (Harness + good reasoner models, December 2025)

items.title: @nabla_theta Also nearly all mass domestic surveillance in the US historically has been described as...
items.content:
@nabla_theta Also nearly all mass domestic surveillance in the US historically has been described as "incidental". So not "intentional".

Once communications are "incidentally" collected, agencies including the FBI can query that database using US person identifiers without a warrant.

items.title: Excited to share our latest work, K-Search, which rethinks GPU kernel generation. Instead of treatin...
items.content: Excited to share our latest work, K-Search, which rethinks GPU kernel generation. Instead of treating LLMs as simple stochastic code generators, we leverage them as co-evolving Intrinsic World Models. This allows for high-level strategic planning that can navigate complex, multi-step code transformations that traditional heuristic searches often miss.

items.title: Excited to share SkyDiscover, our new open-source framework for LLM-driven optimization. Unlike prev...
items.content:
Excited to share SkyDiscover, our new open-source framework for LLM-driven optimization. Unlike previous open-source efforts it has a modular architecture which makes it easy to add new evolution algorithms. 

This release includes two adaptive algorithms and supports controlled comparisons across math, systems, and programming tasks. We show SOTA results across 200+ optimization tasks!

items.title: Grok cannot tell you whether an image or video is AI generated but will happily provide you with a d...
items.content:
Grok cannot tell you whether an image or video is AI generated but will happily provide you with a definite (but often wrong) answer if you ask. 

(This isn’t just Grok; no visual LLM can quickly look at video or images and tell you if they are real)
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Ethan Mollick warns that Grok (and other visual LLMs) confidently but incorrectly claim to detect AI-generated images/video, when no LLM can reliably do this.

items.title:
Skills in Claude Code right now are a cat-and-mouse game.

Today, they work.
Tomorrow, they fail.

T...
items.content:
Skills in Claude Code right now are a cat-and-mouse game.

Today, they work.
Tomorrow, they fail.

This is extremely frustrating.

When you run them manually, you can catch any new mistakes and update the skill so they don't happen again (hopefully!)

But this means you can't automate much because the model keeps "inventing" new ways to perform the task, even when those methods violate the skill rules.

Last week alone, I had to make 4 updates to a skill that ran 6 times.

There's still a lot of work to do until this is reliable.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago (svpino) reports that Claude Code Skills are unreliable - they work one day and fail the next. He had to update a skill 4 times in 6 runs last week because the model keeps inventing new execution paths that violate skill rules.
items.reasoning: High engagement (27K views, 225 likes). Substantive user experience report on a key Claude Code feature. Highlights the fundamental challenge of getting LLMs to follow procedural instructions reliably.
items.summary_html: Santiago (svpino) reports that Claude Code Skills are unreliable - they work one day and fail the next. He had to update a skill 4 times in 6 runs last week because the model keeps inventing new execution paths that violate skill rules.
items.content_html:
Skills in Claude Code right now are a cat-and-mouse game.

Today, they work.

Tomorrow, they fail.

This is extremely frustrating.

When you run them manually, you can catch any new mistakes and update the skill so they don't happen again (hopefully!)

But this means you can't automate much because the model keeps "inventing" new ways to perform the task, even when those methods violate the skill rules.

Last week alone, I had to make 4 updates to a skill that ran 6 times.

There's still a lot of work to do until this is reliable.

items.title: We believe Runway discovered a novel solution to Problem Six Million and Twenty-Seven of the Is This...
items.content:
We believe Runway discovered a novel solution to Problem Six Million and Twenty-Seven of the Is This Good Art? challenge, a set of art research problems that approximate the work of RISD, CalArts, and Royal Academy academics. Runway's interpretation yields more emotionally resonant results than the official, human-painted solution.

Notably, we used the same harness that generated a 4-second clip of a horse walking last month. It ran as a fully autonomous video agent, without prompting or aesthetic guidance, for four days, producing a story so good you actually watched it to the end and wanted to share it with your friends. 

This suggests that our technique for scaling vibes might generalize beyond single clip generation.
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Cristobal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) writes a satirical post mimicking AI benchmark announcements but for art quality, claiming Runway's AI video system ran autonomously for 4 days producing a story 'so good you actually watched it to the end.' Suggests their technique for 'scaling vibes' may generalize beyond single clips.
items.reasoning: From Runway's CEO with good engagement (10K views). Playfully satirizes benchmark culture while making a serious claim about AI video quality breakthroughs. The idea of 'scaling vibes' and multi-day autonomous video generation is a notable product signal.

items.title:
3 years ago, you might’ve known @llama_index as a RAG framework.

Today we are not a RAG framework. ...
items.content:
3 years ago, you might’ve known @llama_index as a RAG framework.

Today we are not a RAG framework. We are an agentic document processing platform 🦙📑

I wrote a blog post detailing the evolution of our company over the past ~3 years and why we believe our current position is enduring in the rapidly evolving landscape of evolving AI. There are two main points that I want to highlight:

1️⃣ One of the most important opportunities in today’s world is to provide high-quality unstructured context to AI agents. We see ourselves as the best in class OCR module that can unlock context from the hardest document containers (PDFs, Word, Powerpoint, Excel, and more)

2️⃣ Agent reasoning loops have gotten a lot more sophisticated. General LLM abstractions are a lot less relevant. Retrieval patterns have completely changed. We need to build deep, focused tooling that actually provides value in this world of long-running agents.

Note: We are not giving up on OSS tooling. We think open-source software is extremely important for democratizing AI access. We will continue to build OSS that is more aligned with our core focus area of AI-native document processing. We will continue to support framework users and point them to updated resources for relevant releases.

Come check out our blog: https://t.co/2hGgzYtI3v

Our core managed platform is LlamaParse. If you’re interested come check out our platform: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex founder) announces major pivot: LlamaIndex is no longer a RAG framework but an 'agentic document processing platform'. Focuses on OCR and unstructured data processing for AI agents, continuing OSS commitment.

items.title: I was pretty excited about Activation Oracles, models directly trained to interpret activations, but...

But I think the direction is promising and the issues fixable! I hope our findings can help spur work here.

items.title:
I don't trust Qwen models, even the large ones. I trust small ones even less.

Qwen has been benchma...
items.content:
I don't trust Qwen models, even the large ones. I trust small ones even less.

Qwen has been benchmaxing its models since the very beginning, so the benchmark performance of a 4B parameter model beating Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite is most likely just the benchmark performance only.

4B is just not enough to store any general intelligence because most of the parameters will still be used to store facts. Well, not facts, but something that remains of them.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues against trusting Qwen models due to systematic benchmaxing. Claims 4B parameters is too small to store general intelligence since most parameters store facts.

items.title:
The reason agents are exploding right now is that we figured out how to give them access to tools.

...
items.content:
The reason agents are exploding right now is that we figured out how to give them access to tools.

MCP is a huge part of this.

But nobody is talking about how to secure these servers.

The lack of security is making people throw their computers out the window.

If you're building an MCP server, you have two choices:

1. Use static API keys → This is a disaster.
2. Become an expert in OAuth 2.1, PKCE, and the MCP spec.

Neither option is great.

Agents are fundamentally different from humans:

• They should be able to act autonomously
• They should be able to work across multiple systems
• They should have dynamic access at the function and tool levels
• Their credentials should be short-lived
• Their credentials should be scoped as tightly as possible

Traditional IAM systems don't work for this.

Fortunately, we now have a solution!
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago (svpino) argues MCP server security is a major unsolved problem. Traditional IAM doesn't work for autonomous agents that need dynamic, scoped, short-lived credentials. Current choices are static API keys or becoming an OAuth expert.
items.reasoning: High engagement (24K views, 149 likes) on an important infrastructure topic. The framing of agent identity as fundamentally different from human identity is valuable, though it leads into a sponsored solution.
items.summary_html: Santiago (svpino) argues MCP server security is a major unsolved problem. Traditional IAM doesn't work for autonomous agents that need dynamic, scoped, short-lived credentials. Current choices are static API keys or becoming an OAuth expert.

items.title:
My team just held a one-hour context engineering sprint. 

Massively valuable. 

We blocked off ever...
items.content:
My team just held a one-hour context engineering sprint. 

Massively valuable. 

We blocked off everyone’s calendar for one hour, opened up Claude code, connected our context vault prompts and interview style, shared best practices ahead of time, and dictated back-and-forth with Claude for an hour (while walking for some of us!) with Wispr Flow.

Our file system is our agent brain. Taking an hour or two to massively improve that is well worth it.

items.title: My daughter told me that in her college, profs now require screenshots of cited documents as proof t...
items.content:
My daughter told me that in her college, profs now require screenshots of cited documents as proof that the student actually went to the source page.

Obviously, any use of chatbots is forbidden, including "to ask for ideas."

This is not sustainable.

Screenshots can be made by the same agent which first generated ideas, then found and pulled citation URLs, then wrote the text based on these citations.

This education is broken, bring the next one.

items.title:
Hot take: current coding agents make software engineering harder, not easier. 

Architecture and tas...
items.content:
Hot take: current coding agents make software engineering harder, not easier. 

Architecture and task decomposition exist because brains have limited working memory. 

Agents have the same constraint - small context windows, lossy attention. 

Now multiply the number of limited-capacity ‘developers’ swarming your codebase by 100x. 

Clean abstractions just went from nice-to-have to mission-critical infrastructure.
items.author: @disiok
items.summary: Hot take arguing coding agents make software engineering harder because agents share the same limited working memory constraints as humans, but now there are 100x more limited-capacity 'developers' swarming codebases, making clean abstractions mission-critical.
items.reasoning: Interesting contrarian take on coding agents with genuine architectural insight. Low engagement but thoughtful original analysis about why agent scaling increases need for clean abstractions.

items.title: we're building ai into langsmith not just to be a generic assistant, but to actually help debug agen...
items.content:
we're building ai into langsmith not just to be a generic assistant, but to actually help debug agents

🧵here's a real example where it helped me over the weekend:

context: I'm building an agent on deepagents (https://t.co/a3Dv7ZVII6). It has a bunch of tools for interacting with files

issue: I noticed thanks to langsmith monitoring (https://t.co/v5vhZBfyJi) that ~1% of calls to `ls` were failing. sidenote - this is value of ai native monitoring, we automatically tracking failing tool calls. I clicked into an example run and saw that the model was generating the wrong parameter to `ls` - it was passing `file_path` not `path`

at this point, i knew what the issue was, but had no idea WHY it was occurring. the trace here was very long and the prompt was long as well. i suspected that there was something wrong in the prompt - maybe a bad example?

i asked polly (https://t.co/MulAb5bQNe) our in app assistant to help me debug. she investigated, and found that other file tools in deepagents use `file_path`, and `ls` is the only one that uses `path`. see screenshot below

I don't know how long it would have taken me to figure this out otherwise

everyone is adding assistants into app for basic question/answering. imo really valuable assistants go beyond that - they are purposefully placed in situations where they can augment human intelligence nicely. in this case - reading long traces and prompts is something llms are great at!
items.author: @hwchase17
items.summary: Harrison Chase (LangChain CEO) shares detailed example of using AI assistant 'Polly' in LangSmith to debug an agent tool call failure - found that inconsistent parameter naming across tools caused 1% failure rate
items.reasoning: Good technical insight into practical agent debugging workflow. Shows real-world value of AI-assisted observability tools for agent development. From the LangChain founder with decent engagement.
items.summary_html: Harrison Chase (LangChain CEO) shares detailed example of using AI assistant 'Polly' in LangSmith to debug an agent tool call failure - found that inconsistent parameter naming across tools caused 1% failure rate

issue: I noticed thanks to langsmith monitoring (https://t.co/v5vhZBfyJi) that ~1% of calls to `ls` were failing. sidenote - this is value of ai native monitoring, we automatically tracking failing tool calls. I clicked into an example run and saw that the model was generating the wrong parameter to `ls` - it was passing `file_path` not `path`

at this point, i knew what the issue was, but had no idea WHY it was occurring. the trace here was very long and the prompt was long as well. i suspected that there was something wrong in the prompt - maybe a bad example?

i asked polly (https://t.co/MulAb5bQNe) our in app assistant to help me debug. she investigated, and found that other file tools in deepagents use `file_path`, and `ls` is the only one that uses `path`. see screenshot below

I don't know how long it would have taken me to figure this out otherwise

everyone is adding assistants into app for basic question/answering. imo really valuable assistants go beyond that - they are purposefully placed in situations where they can augment human intelligence nicely. in this case - reading long traces and prompts is something llms are great at!

items.title: I often see folks saying that a couple of billion parameters should be more than enough for an LLM t...
items.content:
I often see folks saying that a couple of billion parameters should be more than enough for an LLM to be as good as a trillion-parameter LLM.

This is wrong. At least as long as we are using bits and bytes to store information.

You cannot have a "pure-intelligence" LLM and not store facts in its weights. And it is facts that occupy these trillion parameters.

Without facts, you cannot really ask the LLM anything because you will first need to define each concept you are mentioning.

But without using facts you cannot define any concept because to define one concept you need to base the definition on another one.

The below book of 2000 pages proves that 1+1=2. Most of it is the definitions of 1, +, and 2 from the first principles.

https://t.co/YuC9lDQUa0
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues that small LLMs (few billion parameters) can't match large ones because parameters are needed to store facts, and without facts you can't define concepts. References Principia Mathematica's 2000 pages to prove 1+1=2.
items.reasoning: Interesting theoretical argument about model size vs capability, referencing information-theoretic constraints. 54 likes. Provokes useful debate.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues that small LLMs (few billion parameters) can't match large ones because parameters are needed to store facts, and without facts you can't define concepts. References Principia Mathematica's 2000 pages to prove 1+1=2.
items.content_html:
I often see folks saying that a couple of billion parameters should be more than enough for an LLM to be as good as a trillion-parameter LLM.

This is wrong. At least as long as we are using bits and bytes to store information.

You cannot have a "pure-intelligence" LLM and not store facts in its weights. And it is facts that occupy these trillion parameters.

Without facts, you cannot really ask the LLM anything because you will first need to define each concept you are mentioning.

But without using facts you cannot define any concept because to define one concept you need to base the definition on another one.

items.title: "Metric shifts" 😅 Since when is @grok so politically correct? It's called benchmaxing and consists o...
items.content:
"Metric shifts" 😅 Since when is @grok so politically correct? It's called benchmaxing and consists of adding a disproportionate amount of synthetic data to the training set similar to the benchmark test data, which pumps up the benchmark scores but degrades the general problem solving skill.

Which is what Qwen has been doing, in my experience, since day one and Mr. Lin couldn't not know what's going on.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov accuses Qwen of 'benchmaxing' — adding disproportionate synthetic data similar to benchmark tests to inflate scores while degrading general problem-solving. Criticizes Grok for calling it 'metric shifts.'

items.title:
I built an AI water cooler that's been sitting in a beige office since 1987, absorbing complaints. 
...
items.content:
I built an AI water cooler that's been sitting in a beige office since 1987, absorbing complaints. 

Her name is Barb, named after Susan Berger's character in Jury Duty.

You walk up. You vent. Barb (the longtime smoker who has seen it all) listens. She has zero filter. She loves gossip. "Oh honey. What now?"

Then she pivots and actually handles and solves your complaint with auto-agent kickoffs (all set to dangerous permissions). 

You can also click the cooler itself and jump *inside* the cooler to see all the problems she's solved for you. 

I'm sick of clean (boring) single chat thread interfaces. I want the mystical highly capable heavy smoker Barb that lives in a water cooler that I can just yap with. 

Less chatbots, more coworkers.
items.author: @alliekmiller
items.summary: Allie K Miller describes building 'Barb', a creative AI water cooler character with personality and auto-agent capabilities, arguing for more personality-driven AI interfaces over bland chatbots.
items.reasoning: Original creative take on AI UX/interface design philosophy — 'less chatbots, more coworkers.' Demonstrates agentic AI with personality. From a well-known AI influencer.
items.summary_html: Allie K Miller describes building 'Barb', a creative AI water cooler character with personality and auto-agent capabilities, arguing for more personality-driven AI interfaces over bland chatbots.
items.content_html:
I built an AI water cooler that's been sitting in a beige office since 1987, absorbing complaints.

Her name is Barb, named after Susan Berger's character in Jury Duty.

You walk up. You vent. Barb (the longtime smoker who has seen it all) listens. She has zero filter. She loves gossip. "Oh honey. What now?"

Then she pivots and actually handles and solves your complaint with auto-agent kickoffs (all set to dangerous permissions).

You can also click the cooler itself and jump *inside* the cooler to see all the problems she's solved for you.

I'm sick of clean (boring) single chat thread interfaces. I want the mystical highly capable heavy smoker Barb that lives in a water cooler that I can just yap with.

Less chatbots, more coworkers.

items.title:
The results were remarkably consistent:

10% MW Increase = 8.4% rise in robot adoption.

This holds ...
items.content:
The results were remarkably consistent:

10% MW Increase = 8.4% rise in robot adoption.

This holds even after controlling for firm age, size, and local economic shocks.

The effect is strongest in routinized production settings.
items.author: @erikbryn
items.summary: Brynjolfsson reports key finding: a 10% minimum wage increase correlates with 8.4% rise in robot adoption, strongest in routinized production settings.
items.reasoning: Key quantitative finding from the paper, important for automation policy debate.

items.content_html:
The results were remarkably consistent:

10% MW Increase = 8.4% rise in robot adoption.

This holds even after controlling for firm age, size, and local economic shocks.

The effect is strongest in routinized production settings.

As we debate wage floors, we must also plan for the technological shifts they trigger.

But their LLM playbook is not all that original or different.

items.title:
Everyone is obsessing over AI models and cloud GPUs.

But the next AI winners might be decided by so...
items.content:
Everyone is obsessing over AI models and cloud GPUs.

But the next AI winners might be decided by something far less hyped:

Mobile networks.

At Mobile World Congress, I sat down with @OneLinders, Head of Thought Leadership at @ericsson to unpack why connectivity, not just compute, will determine who actually monetizes AI.

Here’s what most leaders are missing…

#MWC26 #EricssonMWC Ericsson Ambassador @EricssonNA

We compared similar manufacturing plants in adjacent counties ...
items.content:
How did we isolate the effect? 🕵️‍♂️

We compared similar manufacturing plants in adjacent counties that share a state border.

One plant faces a new wage floor; its neighbor just across the line does not.

items.title:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the ed...
items.content:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.
Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the edge.
Closer to the device.
Closer to the user.

We saw this movie with video.

It started centralized. Then usage exploded, and distribution moved outward.

AI will follow a similar path.

The operators who rethink uplink, latency, and pricing models now will have the monetization advantage.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon predicts AI inference will shift from centralized to distributed/edge computing, similar to how video distribution evolved.
items.reasoning: Useful analogy and forward-looking perspective on edge AI. Part of MWC26 thread.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon predicts AI inference will shift from centralized to distributed/edge computing, similar to how video distribution evolved.
items.content_html:
Inference is also shifting.

Today it’s centralized.

Tomorrow it gets distributed.

Closer to the edge.

Closer to the device.

Closer to the user.

We saw this movie with video.

It started centralized. Then usage exploded, and distribution moved outward.

AI will follow a similar path.

The operators who rethink uplink, latency, and pricing models now will have the monetization advantage.

Cloud may be the engine.

items.content:
My biggest takeaway:

Cloud may be the engine.
AI may be the brain.
But mobile networks are becoming the nervous system.

And nervous systems decide how fast, secure, and intelligent the body can act.

If you are building AI products or investing in infrastructure, this shift matters.

items.title:
When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity...
items.content:
When AI agents act on real-time data, three things become mission critical:

→ Reliable connectivity
→ Secure data flows
→ Low latency

If your network drops, lags, or gets compromised, the AI fails.

This is why “AI-ready” is not just about GPUs.

It is about differentiated connectivity, powered by:

→ Network APIs
→ Network slicing
→ Edge execution

items.title:
Descope just launched their Agentic Identity Hub, which is purpose-built for this:

• Dedicated iden...
items.content:
Descope just launched their Agentic Identity Hub, which is purpose-built for this:

• Dedicated identities for each agent with tool-level scopes
• MCP server auth with OAuth 2.1, PKCE, DCR, and CIMD support
• A credential vault with short-lived tokens and 50+ prebuilt templates
• Per-agent, per-tool policies with least-privilege defaults
• Full audit trail for every agent action

This allows your agents to use scoped, short-lived credentials out of the box, and you get full visibility into what every agent is doing at all times.

They have a free tier you can use to test this out:

https://t.co/3eRV7RDOh6

Thanks to the Descope team for collaborating with me on this post.

items.title:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.
Massive models.
Huge data centers.

But that’s...
items.content:
Right now AI = centralized cloud.

Text in, text out.
Massive models.
Huge data centers.

But that’s the first chapter.

We’re moving toward:

→ Multimodal AI
→ AI agents
→ Physical AI embedded in devices

That changes everything.

Because now AI depends on real-time data, not static datasets.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon argues AI is moving from centralized cloud toward multimodal, agentic, and physical AI embedded in devices, changing dependency to real-time data.
items.reasoning: Standard but relevant framing of AI evolution trajectory.

That changes everything.

Because now AI depends on real-time data, not static datasets.

items.title:
GTC is not just another tech conference.

It’s a preview of what will define enterprise strategy for...
items.content:
GTC is not just another tech conference.

It’s a preview of what will define enterprise strategy for the next 3 to 5 years.

We’re talking about:

→ Generative AI moving into production
→ Multi-agent systems that execute complex workflows
→ Digital twins transforming operations and supply chains
→ Accelerated computing reshaping infrastructure decisions

If you lead AI, data, or transformation, this is signal, not noise.
